root: ^God speaks in the garden. His voice is not thunder — it is something quieter and more terrible. The voice of absolute authority that does not need to raise itself to be obeyed.
^He turns first to the serpent — the creature whose form Satan borrowed, now cursed for the role it played.
^Because thou hast done this, thou art cursed above all cattle, and above every beast of the field. Upon thy belly shalt thou go, and dust shalt thou eat all the days of thy life.

^The serpent writhes. Its legs — for in Eden, serpents had legs — wither and fold beneath it. It falls to the ground, its body contorting into the crawling thing it will be forevermore. The other animals watch in silence. They do not understand, but they feel it — the first cruelty, the first punishment, the first sign that Paradise has rules with teeth.
^And I will put enmity between thee and the woman, and between thy seed and her seed. It shall bruise thy head, and thou shalt bruise his heel.

^Satan, hidden in the Tree of Life, hears this and stiffens. Her seed. A prophecy — veiled, distant, but unmistakable. Something will come from Eve's line that will strike at him. A bruise to the head. Not a killing blow, perhaps. But a wound.
^Unto the woman He said: I will greatly multiply thy sorrow and thy conception. In sorrow thou shalt bring forth children, and thy desire shall be to thy husband, and he shall rule over thee.

^Eve bows her head. The words fall on her like stones. Sorrow. Pain. Subjection. The freedom she wielded this morning — the freedom to choose, to act, to reach for the fruit — is already being reshaped into chains she will wear for all the generations that follow.

^And unto Adam He said: Because thou hast hearkened unto the voice of thy wife, and hast eaten of the tree — cursed is the ground for thy sake. In sorrow shalt thou eat of it all the days of thy life. Thorns and thistles shall it bring forth to thee. In the sweat of thy face shalt thou eat bread, till thou return unto the ground — for dust thou art, and unto dust shalt thou return.

^Adam does not speak. He stands with his head bowed, his fig-leaf garment rough against his skin, and accepts the sentence. There is nothing else to do. The verdict is just — he knows it, even as it destroys him.
^(The judgment is fair. They chose freely, and consequences follow choice.)
^(The judgment is cruel. God made them capable of falling, then punished them for it.)
^(The judgment is both — justice and mercy intertwined, as they always are with God.)

c-0: ^God gave one command. One prohibition in an ocean of permission. And they broke it — not in ignorance but in full knowledge, not by accident but by choice. If freedom means anything, it means accepting the weight of what you choose.
^Adam and Eve chose knowledge. Now they have it. And knowledge, as Satan learned long ago, comes at a price that can never be repaid.
c-1: ^He made them curious and then forbade curiosity. He made them free and then punished freedom. He placed the tree in the centre of the garden — not hidden, not locked away, but right there, beautiful and beckoning — and then condemned them for reaching out.

^Satan, listening from his perch, almost laughs. This is the God he rebelled against. This is the justice he refused to serve. And now Adam and Eve are learning what he learned — that the Almighty's love comes with conditions, and the conditions have teeth.
c-2: ^The curse is real. The pain will be real. The death that waits at the end of every human life will be terribly, inescapably real. God does not soften the sentence or pretend the consequences are less than they are.

^But buried in the curse is a seed of mercy — her seed shall bruise thy head. A promise. A distant, veiled, almost invisible promise that this is not the end. That something will come — someone — who will strike back at the serpent, who will undo what was done today.
^It is not much. It is everything.

the_son: ^In Heaven, the judgment has already been prepared. Before the foundation of the world — before Eden, before the angels, before the first light divided from the first darkness — God knew this moment would come. And the answer was ready.

^He comes not in wrath but in sorrow. He comes not as a judge but as a servant — though He is both. The garden parts before Him. The trees bow. Even the cursed serpent stills its writhing as the Son of God walks through Paradise for the last time it will be Paradise.

^He finds Adam and Eve huddled in the shadow of the fig trees, wrapped in their crude garments of leaves. They cannot look at Him. Shame — that new, crippling invention — pins their eyes to the ground.
^The Son kneels. He kneels before them — the Creator before the created, the infinite before the finite, the eternal before the mortal.
^And He removes their fig leaves, gently, and clothes them in garments of skin.
^(An animal died for those skins. The first death in Eden — to cover human shame.)
^(The act of clothing them is mercy. They do not deserve it, but grace has never been about deserving.)

c-0: ^Somewhere in the garden, a lamb lies still. Its blood soaks into the earth — the first blood ever spilled in Eden, the first death in a world that was made for life. Adam and Eve wear its skin without understanding what it cost.

^But the Son understands. He looks at the garments He has made, and He sees forward — through all the ages of blood and sacrifice that will follow, to the day when He Himself will be the lamb, when His own skin will be torn to clothe a naked world.
c-1: ^They sinned. They fell. They broke the one rule they were given. And God's response — before the exile, before the angel with the flaming sword, before the long road of human suffering begins — is to kneel in the dirt and dress them.
^It is an act so tender it seems almost out of place in the wreckage. But this is who God is, even now. Even here. Even after everything.
^Adam looks up for the first time. His eyes meet the Son's. And in that look — grief meeting grace, shame meeting love — something passes between them that has no name. It will later be called hope.

sin_and_death: ^Far away, at the gates of Hell, Sin feels a change in the fabric of the world. The great gates — those adamantine doors she has guarded since her birth — begin to vibrate. The lock trembles. The hinges groan.
^She turns to Death — her son, her shadow, the formless horror that she birthed in agony at the very moment of the Fall from Heaven.
^Do you feel it, my son? The way is open. Our father has succeeded.
^Death says nothing. Death never speaks. But it moves — a vast, shapeless hunger lurching toward the gates — and Sin understands.

^A bridge. A causeway across Chaos — that formless void between Hell and the created world. They work with impossible speed, paving the way with ice and stone and solidified darkness, creating a road where none existed, connecting Hell to Earth in a permanent highway of corruption.
^(The bridge is an obscenity — Hell's corruption bleeding into creation.)
^(The bridge is inevitable — the Fall opened a door that can never be closed.)

c-0: ^Where they build, the void freezes. Where they pass, the formless takes shape — but wrong shape, twisted shape, matter organized by malice rather than love. The bridge is a scar across the face of creation, a permanent wound connecting Hell's darkness to Earth's new fragility.
^Sin walks the completed bridge with something like pride. Death follows, consuming everything in its path — even the bridge itself seems to corrode beneath that shapeless tread.
^Behind them, the gates of Hell stand open. Through them pours a cold wind that smells of sulphur and despair. It will blow across the Earth for all the ages to come.
c-1: ^The bridge was always going to exist. The moment Eve bit the fruit — the moment innocence died and knowledge was born — the road between Hell and Earth became a certainty. Sin and Death are not creating something new. They are revealing what the Fall made real.

^Every sin from this day forward will be a footstep on this bridge. Every act of cruelty, every betrayal, every lie — all of them walking the highway that Sin and Death have paved between damnation and the world.
^The bridge is complete. Hell and Earth are joined. And Death enters the world for the first time, silent and patient and infinitely hungry.

satan_returns: ^He flies the bridge that Sin and Death have built, crossing Chaos in triumph where once he struggled through its terrors alone. The gates of Hell stand open for him — a hero's welcome.
^Pandemonium blazes with infernal light. The fallen angels have gathered — all of them, the entire host, filling the great hall that was built to house their defiance. They wait for their king.

^Satan enters. He takes his throne. He opens his mouth to speak — to announce his victory, to describe how he corrupted God's creation with a single fruit, how he brought down Paradise with nothing but words.
^His tongue thickens. His words twist. Where a triumphant speech should pour forth, there is only a dry, rasping hiss. Satan looks down at his hands — and sees scales.

^The transformation ripples through Pandemonium like a wave. Satan shrinks, contorts, falls from his throne and writhes on the floor as his body reshapes itself into the form of a monstrous serpent. And around him — screaming, thrashing, crying out in horror — every fallen angel suffers the same fate.

^The hall of Pandemonium fills with serpents. Where proud angels stood, now a pit of writhing, hissing, crawling things covers the floor. They try to speak and produce only sibilance. They try to fly and can only slither.
^God's punishment. Not for Eve, not for Adam, but for them — the architects of the Fall, now wearing the shape of their crime for all eternity.

^A grove of trees appears in the midst of Hell — beautiful trees laden with fruit that looks exactly like the fruit of the Tree of Knowledge. The serpent-angels lunge for it, desperate, starving, driven by a hunger they cannot control.
^They bite. The fruit turns to ash in their mouths.
